results.title: [Literature Review] WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents with Hybrid Interfaces
results.content:
WeaveBench is a novel, long-horizon, real-world benchmark designed to evaluate computer-use agents (CUAs) operating with hybrid interfaces, specifically visual desktop control (GUI) and command-line/code execution (CLI/code). Existing benchmarks often assess these interfaces in isolation, failing to test an agent's ability to orchestrate complex workflows that necessitate interleaved operations across both modalities. WeaveBench addresses this critical gap by providing 114 tasks spanning 8 real-world work domains, each requiring agents to seamlessly combine GUI observations/actions with CLI/code operations within a single execution trajectory. Tasks are grounded in genuine user requests and publicly verifiable artifacts. [...] The benchmark's core methodology is built upon rigorous task admission criteria and a sophisticated evaluation protocol.
Task Admission Criteria: A WeaveBench task must satisfy three properties:

Task Construction: Tasks are built through a four-stage pipeline:

Evaluation Protocol: Trajectory-Aware Agentic Judge:
Traditional outcome-only grading is insufficient for hybrid rollouts, as final deliverables can be synthesized, metrics hard-coded, or states reached through specification-violating tool use. WeaveBench employs a trajectory-aware agentic judge (an isolated OpenClaw agent, fixed at GPT-5.5) that re-fetches evidence over multiple turns using file, image, and shell tools.
The scoring follows a five-layer pipeline: [...] `task_completion`
`deliverable_correctness`
`evidence_authenticity`
`tool_use_correctness`
`efficiency_robustness`

Metrics: WeaveBench reports two complementary metrics:

Experimental Setup:
Agents operate in a containerized Linux VM booting from a frozen snapshot, with restricted network access and per-task budgets. The hybrid harness adds a minimal GUI plugin (one `screenshot` primitive and nine `pyautogui`-backed actuation primitives like `click`, `type`) to existing CLI-agent runtimes like OpenClaw.
Experiments involve a model-API sweep (e.g., GPT-5.x, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro) on a fixed OpenClaw runtime, and a cross-harness sweep where the strongest APIs are evaluated across different deployed runtimes (Codex CLI, Claude Code, Hermes).

results.title: [PDF] WEAVE: Unleashing and Benchmarking the In-context Interleaved
results.content:
WEAVEBench is a human-annotated benchmark of 100 items with 480 images, featuring a hybrid VLM judge eval-uation framework with four metrics that assess alignment with reference images, fidelity to original images, and cor-rectness of editing instructions. The benchmark evaluates models’ capabilities in multi-turn generation, visual memory, and world-knowledge reasoning across diverse domains, in-cluding science, creation, logic, and games. WEAVEBench reveals that current models struggle with in-context inter-leaved generation and exhibit performance degradation as content length increases, indicating substantial room for im-provement. [...] WEAVEBench is annotated by individuals with graduate-level STEM degrees. It comprises 100 items across 16 task categories, incorporating both multi-turn editing tasks re-quiring visual memory and challenges demanding world knowledge (e.g. cultural contexts, physical phenomena, and chemical processes). As illustrated in Figure 2, tasks include generating examples involving the Tokyo Tower and demon-strating comprehension of traffic signal reactions. All initial images are sourced from real-world data. During evaluation, the model generates outputs autonomously at each turn. For the error accumulation ablation (Section 4.1), we provide human-annotated references produced by SOTA models. [...] • We present WEAVEBench, the first human-annotated benchmark for interleaved multimodal comprehension and generation, featuring 100 carefully designed cases with 480 images and a hybrid VLM judge evaluation frame-work that assesses multi-turn generation, visual memory, and world-knowledge reasoning.
• Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that training on WEAVE-100k significantly improves perfor-mance on established benchmarks and facilitates the emer-gence of visual memory capabilities, while evaluation on WEAVEBench reveals persistent limitations in multi-turn, context-aware generation.

results.title: WeaveBench: Testing Hybrid Computer-Use Agents
results.content:
In this AI Research Roundup episode, Alex discusses the paper: 'WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents with Hybrid Interfaces' WeaveBench is a new long-horizon benchmark designed to evaluate computer-use agents across hybrid interfaces, bridging graphical user interfaces (GUIs) and command-line interfaces (CLIs). Traditional evaluations often isolate these capabilities, allowing agents to bypass GUI requirements through programmatic shortcuts. WeaveBench addresses this with 114 complex tasks across 8 real-world domains like DevOps and CAD, requiring deep coordination and state tracking between tools. Additionally, the authors developed a trajectory-aware agentic judge that inspects workspace states to prevent shortcut behaviors and reward hacking. This [...] to prevent shortcut behaviors and reward hacking. This benchmark provides a highly realistic environment to test how effectively multimodal models can navigate real-world operating systems. results.title: WEAVE: Unleashing and Benchmarking the In-context Interleaved Comprehension and Generation
results.content:
## WEAVEBench: A New Evaluation Standard

Image 2: WEAVEBench examples across different domains_Figure 2: WEAVEBench spans 16 diverse categories across natural science, creation, and logic domains, requiring models to demonstrate visual memory, world knowledge reasoning, and multi-turn generation capabilities._

WEAVEBench represents the first human-annotated benchmark specifically designed for evaluating interleaved multimodal comprehension and generation. Created by individuals with graduate-level STEM backgrounds, the benchmark encompasses 100 tasks across 16 categories, spanning natural science domains (optics, physics, astronomy, biology, chemistry), creative applications (story generation, image editing, fusion), and logical reasoning (spatial reasoning, mathematics, games). [...] Summary

Problem

Method

Results

Takeaways

Abstract

WEAVE introduces a comprehensive suite, including a large-scale dataset (WEAVE-100k) and a human-annotated benchmark (WEAVEBench), designed to evaluate and enhance Unified Multimodal Models' (UMMs) abilities in multi-turn, context-dependent image comprehension and generation. Training UMMs on WEAVE-100k improves visual memory and long-horizon reasoning, while WEAVEBench exposes current models' limitations in these areas.

## Table of Contents

### Understanding WEAVE: Advancing Multi-Turn Image Generation and Comprehension

### The Problem with Current Approaches

### WEAVE's Dual Contribution

### WEAVEBench: A New Evaluation Standard

### Data Creation Pipeline and Quality Assurance

results.content:
WeaveBench evaluates 4 core metrics:

| Metric | Code | Description | Requires Text | Requires Image |
 ---  --- 
| Key Point Correctness | KP | Measures whether the edited image satisfies the specified editing requirements. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Visual Consistency | VC | Ensures non-target elements remain unchanged and maintains consistency with the original image. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Image Quality | IQ | Evaluates the overall quality of the generated image. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Accuracy | ACC | Measures the correctness of the reasoning result for comprehension tasks. | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

Consider adding your results to our leaderboard at Weave Project Page. Please contact weichow@qq.com for submission details!

When using multi-turn conversations, each number index should be replaced once with `Image #{idx}<image>\n`. For single-turn, simply replace directly.

results.title: WeaveBench | Long-Horizon Hybrid-Interface Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents
results.content:
Deployed CUA runtimes already integrate GUI control, CLI/code execution, browsers, and external tools inside one agent loop. Existing benchmarks test these channels in isolation, leaving the orchestration layer under-measured. WeaveBench is built around tasks where success requires the agent to weave both channels together.

### GUI and CLI are complementary, not interchangeable.

GUIs expose rendered, transient, spatial state — canvases, dialogs, visual feedback. CLI/code expose structured, scriptable, persistent state — source files, configs, logs, services. Real workflows braid them: observe in the GUI, verify in the shell, edit in code, re-render, re-observe.

Real Ubuntu desktop 114 sandboxed tasks 8 work domains 4 deployed harnesses Trajectory-aware judge [...] WeaveBench 
 Release v0.1 · 114 tasks

A long-horizon, real-world benchmark for computer-use agents with hybrid interfaces

# WeaveBench

114 tasks across 8 real-world work domains, each requiring an agent to weave GUI observation with CLI/code execution within a single trajectory. Rollouts run inside a real Ubuntu sandbox; a trajectory-aware Agent-as-a-Judge audits eight quality dimensions and zeros credit on any of nine shortcut patterns (synthetic screenshots, hard-coded metrics, mock services…).

Core Idea Pipeline Results  Code Repo   arXiv:2606.09426   🤗 Dataset

 One-command setup bash scripts/setup.sh Installs deps + downloads dataset + 28 GB qcow2.  →

Figure 1 from the paper

## Three real-world workflows that require interleaved GUI & CLI. [...] WeaveBench dataset overview: taxonomy of 114 tasks across 8 domains and 23 subcategories; per-task GUI/CLI channel-switch distribution; per-task tool-call rollout length. 

Figure 3 from the paper. (a) Taxonomy of 114 tasks across 8 domains and 23 subcategories. (b) GUI↔CLI channel switches per task (median 16) — the degree of channel interleaving. (c) Rollout length measured by tool calls in the trajectory (median 76, max 471).

results.title: Paper page - WEAVE: Unleashing and Benchmarking the In-context Interleaved Comprehension and Generation
results.content:
Recent advances in unified multimodal models (UMMs) have enabled impressive progress in visual comprehension and generation. However, existing datasets and benchmarks focus primarily on single-turn interactions, failing to capture the multi-turn, context-dependent nature of real-world image creation and editing. To address this gap, we present WEAVE, the first suite for in-context interleaved cross-modality comprehension and generation. Our suite consists of two complementary parts. WEAVE-100k is a large-scale dataset of 100K interleaved samples spanning over 370K dialogue turns and 500K images, covering comprehension, editing, and generation tasks that require reasoning over historical context. WEAVEBench is a human-annotated benchmark with 100 tasks based on 480 images, featuring a [...] benchmark with 100 tasks based on 480 images, featuring a hybrid VLM judger evaluation framework based on both the reference image and the combination of the original image with editing instructions that assesses models' abilities in multi-turn generation, visual memory, and world-knowledge reasoning across diverse domains. Experiments demonstrate that training on WEAVE-100k enables vision comprehension, image editing, and comprehension-generation collaboration capabilities. Furthermore, it facilitates UMMs to develop emergent visual-memory capabilities, while extensive evaluations on WEAVEBench expose the persistent limitations and challenges of current approaches in multi-turn, context-aware image generation and editing. We believe WEAVE provides a view and foundation for studying [...] Hugging Face's logo

# WEAVE: Unleashing and Benchmarking the In-context Interleaved Comprehension and Generation

## Abstract

WEAVE introduces a comprehensive suite including a large dataset and a benchmark to assess and improve multi-turn, context-dependent image generation and editing in unified multimodal models.

OpenRouter aliases drift over time. To reproduce paper numbers, pin both the dataset SHA and the model snapshot ids — see `docs/REPRODUCE.md` in the code repo for the canonical SHA, per-table model ids, and per-table commands.

`docs/REPRODUCE.md`
`export WEAVEBENCH_DATASET_REVISION=<full sha from docs/REPRODUCE.md>`
